planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil in hills; Collective Farm Woman (Kolkhoznitsa) is the most widely grown open-pollinated melon in Russia and Eastern Europe, developed in the Soviet era and still the standard home-garden melon across the former Soviet republics — extraordinarily flavorful despite the unglamorous name.

directions: Sow 3–4 seeds per hill after last frost in well-amended, well-drained soil; thin to 2 strongest plants per hill; direct vines along rows or up a strong trellis. Collective Farm Woman (Russian: Kolkhoznitsa) is a Ukrainian/Russian heirloom muskmelon introduced in 1930 that became the standard garden melon across the Soviet Union and is still the most widely grown open-pollinated melon in Russia and Ukraine; the small (1–2 lb) round to oval fruits ripen to creamy-white to yellow with thin, smooth skin and extremely aromatic, sweet, creamy-white flesh that has an exceptional honey-floral fragrance when perfectly ripe. Despite its short ripening window once mature, the flavor is outstanding — rich, sweet, and intensely perfumed. One of the earliest-maturing muskmelons (75–85 days from transplant), making it excellent for short-season northern gardens and for early production in the South and Gulf Coast. Harvest when skin turns creamy-yellow and the blossom end softens slightly.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-amended soil; Tigger melon is a striking ornamental-edible Middle Eastern/Central Asian heirloom with bright yellow skin dramatically striped and splashed with crimson and orange, producing small to medium fruits with cream to pale orange flesh and a sweet, spicy, slightly fruity flavor.

directions: Sow in hills after last frost in warm, well-amended, well-drained soil; thin to 2 plants per hill and allow vines to sprawl or train on a sturdy trellis with sling support for fruits. Tigger is a Central Asian or Middle Eastern heirloom melon with one of the most striking exteriors in the melon world — the smooth, round to oval, medium-sized (1–2 lb) fruits are a brilliant golden-yellow with dramatic crimson-to-orange zigzag stripes, spots, and splashes that make them resemble the fictional tiger character they are named for. The flesh is cream-colored to pale orange with a sweet, spicy, slightly fruity aroma that is pleasant rather than intensely musky; more of an ornamental conversation-piece melon than a flavor standout, but still delicious and perfectly edible. Extremely heat-tolerant and productive in hot climates; outstanding as a farmers market novelty and ornamental edible.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, deeply amended soil; Ananas (French for 'pineapple') melon is a Middle Eastern and Mediterranean heirloom producing large (3–5 lb), netted, oval fruits with intensely aromatic, spicy-sweet orange or white flesh that many consider among the finest-flavored of all melons — widely grown from Israel to France.

directions: Sow in hills or rows after last frost in deeply enriched, well-drained soil; Ananas is a large-fruited type needing 6–8 feet of row space or a very strong trellis with hammock-style fruit supports. Ananas melon (ananas is French and many Mediterranean languages for pineapple) is a widely grown Middle Eastern, Israeli, and Mediterranean heirloom producing large, oval to round, netted fruits (3–7 lb) with a sweet, spicy, intensely aromatic fragrance when ripe that has been compared to pineapple, vanilla, and spiced honey. The flesh can be white (in the traditional Israeli 'Ananas Yokneam' type) or orange, always with a distinctive rich, complex fragrance that perfumes a room when a ripe fruit is present. Several regional strains exist: French Ananas (yellow netted, orange flesh), Israeli Yokneam (white smooth skin, white flesh), and American strains. Generally large-fruited and somewhat heat-sensitive; best grown in dry conditions as wet weather can dilute flavor. Considered by some the finest-flavored muskmelon type in existence.

common_name: Noir des Carmes Melon (Black Rock Melon)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-amended soil; Noir des Carmes is a French heirloom melon dating to at least 1883, one of the earliest-maturing Charentais-type melons, with dark green to almost black skin that ripens to orange-yellow and contains brilliant, deep-orange, extraordinarily fragrant flesh — one of the finest European muskmelons.

directions: Sow in hills or rows after last frost in warm, enriched, well-drained soil; Noir des Carmes performs best with reduced watering as the fruits approach maturity — drying out slightly at the end concentrates the sugars and perfume. Noir des Carmes (Black of the Carmelites, named for the dark-robed Carmelite friars who reportedly cultivated it) is a French heirloom muskmelon first listed in catalogs in 1883 and described by Vilmorin-Andrieux in their famous 1885 The Vegetable Garden; the young fruits are dark green to nearly black (the 'noir'), ripening to orange-yellow at maturity with a pronounced perfume. Inside, the flesh is deep orange, very sweet, and extraordinarily fragrant in the way characteristic of the finest Charentais-type melons of Poitou-Charentes. One of the earliest European muskmelons (70–80 days from seed), making it suitable for shorter seasons. Somewhat rare but increasingly available from specialty seed companies.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, deeply enriched soil; Montreal Muskmelon is a large, historic Canadian heirloom that dominated North American melon production from the 1860s through the 1920s and was once the most celebrated melon on the continent, shipped to New York and Boston in elegant straw-packed wooden crates as a luxury product.

directions: Sow in deeply enriched, well-drained hills after last frost; Montreal Muskmelon is a large, demanding variety that needs rich soil and ample space — traditional Montreal growers would excavate three-foot-deep planting holes and fill them with aged manure before planting. Montreal Muskmelon was the most celebrated North American melon from roughly 1850 to 1920, grown on the Island of Montreal (particularly in the Rivière-des-Prairies area) in intensively managed, manure-enriched beds and shipped to Montreal, New York, and Boston as a luxury fruit commanding premium prices; the Ferme Onésime Pépin operation shipped 11,000 melons daily at peak production. The large (10–15 lb), deeply ribbed, heavily netted fruits have orange flesh with a rich, intensely sweet, musky perfume that made it the yardstick by which all other melons were judged. It went extinct in commercial production by the 1940s but was revived from Seed Savers Exchange members; now being recultivated by slow food advocates in Montreal.
